metadata.title: Claude, Observed
metadata.note: COLD OPEN LAW: B00 = ClaudeComposerAsk. IN-FOR-BEAR LAW: B00 opens 'This is Liam, in for Bear.' ASK→RESULT LAW: B01=ask micro-beat→B02=Manim F1; B03=ask→B04=Manim F2; B05=ask→B06=Manim F3; B07=ask→B08=Manim F4a→B09=Manim F4b; B10=ask→B11=Manim F5; B12=ask→B13=Manim F6. HANDOFF LAW: B16='Your turn.' OUTRO LAW: B17=ClaudeTitleOutro. FIGURE RULES: Figures 1–2 qualitative by design (no invented numbers). Source: mba-management ch.1.
metadata.description_blurb: In 1924 a factory experiment broke. The independent variable predicted productivity in the same direction regardless of which way it moved. That is not a finding — that is a broken experiment. What it revealed changed management forever.

beats.role_note: COLD OPEN LAW — ClaudeComposerAsk. IN-FOR-BEAR LAW: first breath introduces Liam. Output lines show the key insight.
beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. In 1924, researchers at the Hawthorne Works — a telephone relay factory outside Chicago — turned the lights up. Output went up. They turned the lights down. Output went up again. They returned the lights to the original level. Output went up once more. An independent variable that predicts productivity in exactly the same direction regardless of which way it moves. That is not a finding. That is a broken experiment.

props.command: In 1924, researchers at the Hawthorne Works raised the lights and productivity went up. Then they dimmed the lights — and productivity went up again. How is that possible? What was the actual independent variable, and what did this broken experiment actually discover about how work and organizations function?
output: The variable was attention, not light.
A factory is a social system.

beats.role_note: ASK→RESULT LAW: composer ask before the Manim chart. SPARK-LINE LAW: inner composer beat needs a greeting/cue.
beats.narration_text: Show me what that data looks like — three interventions, one line, all rising.

props.command: Draw a step chart of the Hawthorne relay experiment. X-axis: three interventions labeled Brighter, Dimmer, Restored. Y-axis: output (qualitative — no invented numbers). The output line steps UP at every intervention. Above the x-axis, a small lamp glyph that brightens, dims, then resets. On the third step — unchanged light, output still rising — add a terracotta callout: 'That is not a finding.' Then fade in an eye symbol (the hidden observer) behind the lamp.

beats.role_note: Manim: qualitative step chart — no invented numbers. Under 30s per spec. Source: Ch.1 para 1-4.
beats.narration_text: Three interventions. Brighter — up. Dimmer — up. Restored — up again. The lamp brightens, dims, resets. The line does the same thing every time. The terracotta moment: the third intervention, unchanged light, and the output still climbs. Then the reveal — the variable was never the light.

beats.role_note: ASK→RESULT LAW: ask before the reanalysis Manim.
beats.narration_text: Elton Mayo said observation was the whole explanation. Later researchers went back to the raw data. Show me what they actually found.

props.command: Draw the reanalysis of the Hawthorne data. Show Mayo's original single 'OBSERVATION' block fracturing into four labeled factors: worker selection, supervision changes, wage incentives, and observation — with the observation piece visibly smaller than the others. No invented percentages. Caption: 'Real. Smaller than the story. Still the right lesson.'

beats.role_note: Manim: qualitative proportion-free block diagram. Chapter gives NO variance percentages — labeled blocks only. Source: Ch.1 para 5.
beats.narration_text: Later researchers went back to the raw data. Mayo's single observation block fractures into four factors. Worker selection — which workers were chosen. Supervision changes — how oversight shifted mid-experiment. Wage incentives — adjustments introduced partway through. And the observation effect itself, visible smaller than the legend claimed. Real. Smaller than the story. Still the right lesson.

beats.role_note: ASK→RESULT LAW: ask before the five functions Manim.
beats.narration_text: Show me the five functions of work — and what happens when you drop a raise on each one.

props.command: Draw five cards revealing in sequence: Economic, Social, Status, Identity, Actualization — each with a one-line description. Between cards, show paraphrased Terkel vignettes: a steelworker pointing to a house he built; a sanitation driver whose wife was proud; a receptionist dismissed at a party. Add a stat chip: $45,000 in meaningful work beats $65,000 in pointless work. Then animate a raise token dropped onto each card — it only repairs Economic. Terracotta on the failed repairs.
props.segment: Figure 3 — The Five Functions of Work

beats.act: FIGURE 3 — The Five Functions of Work
beats.role_note: Manim: five card stack + raise diagnostic flip. Source: Ch.1 'What Work Actually Does'. $45k/$65k figure from chapter.
beats.narration_text: Five functions. Economic: income and material security. Social: belonging — strip work away and many find themselves profoundly isolated, even when remote. Status: your occupation signals your social position. Identity: work accumulates evidence that you are capable — Freud's phrase, work binds a person to reality. Actualization: the rarest. The chapter's one hard number: forty-five thousand dollars in meaningful work beats sixty-five thousand in pointless work on reported satisfaction. Drop a raise on each card. It repairs exactly one. If the identity function is broken, money is not the diagnosis.

beats.role_note: ASK→RESULT LAW: ask before the management pyramid and skills triangle.
beats.narration_text: Management is four activities at three levels — and the skills required shift as you climb.

props.command: Draw the management pyramid: executive at top, middle in the middle, first-line at the bottom. Each level shows qualitative proportions of how time splits across planning, organizing, directing, controlling — executives mostly plan, first-line mostly directs. Then beside it, draw the skills triangle: Technical, Human, Conceptual — showing how technical skills shrink and conceptual skills grow as you move from first-line to executive, while human skills stay constant. Mark the promotion trap at both transition points with terracotta annotation rings.

beats.role_note: Manim: pyramid with activity time bands per level. Qualitative proportions only — chapter gives none as exact numbers. Source: Ch.1 'What Managers Actually Do'.
beats.narration_text: Four activities: planning, organizing, directing, controlling. An executive mostly plans — sets strategy, allocates capital. A first-line manager mostly directs — clarifies tasks, removes obstacles. Middle management translates. The proportions shift by level.

beats.role_note: Manim: skills triangle with marker climbing. Terracotta rings at promotion trap transitions. Source: Ch.1 'What Managers Actually Do'.
beats.narration_text: Three skills: technical, human, conceptual. Technical is the entry price — you cannot set realistic goals for work you do not understand. The trap runs at both transitions: promoted for the last job's skills, failing for want of the next job's. The excellent engineer becomes the manager who reviews every line of code, solves problems themselves, and loses the team. Each level requires different skills — not more of the same.

beats.role_note: ASK→RESULT LAW: ask before the management timeline.
beats.narration_text: A century of management thought — what each school got right, and what it missed.

props.command: Draw a left-to-right timeline from 1890s to present: Scientific Management, Human Relations, Systems Thinking (1960s), Contingency, Evidence-Based Management. Each school gets two chips: its genuine contribution and its key limitation. Mark Hawthorne 1924-1932 as the terracotta pivot between Scientific Management and Human Relations. At the end, side-by-side chips for MBTI ('no predictive validity for job performance') vs Big Five ('predicts modestly').
props.segment: Figure 5 — A Century of Management Thought

beats.act: FIGURE 5 — A Century of Management Thought
beats.role_note: Manim: timeline sweep. MBTI claim from chapter: 'essentially no predictive validity for job performance'. Big Five: 'predicts modestly'. Source: Ch.1 'How Management Thinking Evolved'.
beats.narration_text: Scientific management — Taylor, 1890s. Measure, standardize, reward compliance. Real gains. Human cost. Hawthorne 1924 to 1932 marks the pivot. Human relations: satisfaction matters — blind spot was assuming worker and management interests align; they often diverge. Systems thinking: everything connected, limited on what to DO. Contingency theory: it depends — true, not immediately helpful. Evidence-based management: an aspiration. One honest example — the Myers-Briggs is used in a large proportion of major organizations and has essentially no predictive validity for job performance. The Big Five predicts performance modestly. An evidence-based manager notices the distinction.

beats.role_note: ASK→RESULT LAW: ask before the five-block diagnostic.
beats.narration_text: The five-block diagnostic — one symptom, five root causes, and the wrong-diagnosis loop.

props.command: Draw five interlocking blocks as a connected system: Individuals & Groups, Tasks & Technology, Organization Design, Processes, Management (the connective tissue). Show one symptom card 'output down, not responding to feedback' branching into five hypothesis chips — one per block. Animate the wrong-diagnosis path: Block 1 assumed → escalation → termination → same symptom card reappears on the next hire. Terracotta on the loop. Final overlay: Hawthorne mapped onto the blocks — researchers testing Block 2, workers responding to Block 4.

beats.role_note: Manim: five-block connected system + wrong-diagnosis loop + Hawthorne mapping. Source: Ch.1 'A Model for Understanding Organizations'.
beats.narration_text: Five interlocking blocks: individuals and groups, tasks and technology, organization design, processes, and management as the connective tissue. One symptom: output down, not responding to feedback. One hypothesis per block. Diagnose Block 1 — the person is disengaged by choice — when the truth is Block 3: their work waits invisibly on another department, no visibility, no resolution. You escalate the wrong treatment. They leave. The next person in that role breaks the same way. Hawthorne, restated: the researchers were testing Block 2. The workers were responding to Block 4.

beats.role_note: SLATE: landing narration. No UI here — ILLUSTRATE LAW. Simple text card or slate.
beats.visual_intent: Cream page, centered serif text — the chapter's core idea as a poster: 'Management is not a procedure you apply to people.'
beats.narration_text: Management is not a procedure you apply to people. It is creating the conditions — social, structural, motivational — in which people are able to do their best work, and choose to. The researchers thought they were testing light bulbs. One aside: AI models also behave differently when they know they're being evaluated. The Hawthorne effect did not stay in the factory. Liam, in for Bear.

beats.role_note: ClaudeVerdictArtifact — recap. Narration starts 'Let's recap with Claude.' (no prior Bear body to thank). Source: all of Ch.1.
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. In 1924 a broken experiment revealed that the social system around work is more powerful than the physical conditions. The reanalysis told a smaller story — but the lesson stands: the wrong block was being examined. Work provides five functions money cannot fully substitute. Different levels require different skills — not more of the same. And when something breaks, examine all five blocks before concluding it is a Block 1 problem.

artifactLines: A broken experiment revealed that the social system around work is more powerful than its physical conditions.
The reanalysis told a smaller story — but the lesson stands: the researchers were examining the wrong block.
Work provides five functions money cannot fully substitute — diagnose which one is broken before reaching for the raise.
Each management level requires different skills, not more of the same — the promotion trap runs at both transitions.

beats.role_note: HANDOFF LAW: 'Your turn.' greeting. Prompt READ ALOUD verbatim + discussed. Second-to-last beat.
beats.narration_text: Your move. Here is the prompt. 'Someone on my team has disengaged — output down, not responding to feedback. Before I assume it's them, walk me through the five-block diagnostic: give me one hypothesis and one testable question for the person, the task, the org design, the processes, and my own management.' What you are looking for: whether Claude holds all five blocks simultaneously without defaulting to Block 1. It usually does. Take this into your session.

props.command: Someone on my team has disengaged — output down, not responding to feedback. Before I assume it's them, walk me through the five-block diagnostic: give me one hypothesis and one testable question for the person, the task, the org design, the processes, and my own management.

beats.role_note: OUTRO LAW: ClaudeTitleOutro restates episode title. Title: 'Claude, Observed' — double meaning lands here.

props.title: Claude, Observed
props.subline: the Hawthorne effect and what it revealed
